summary: Fowler describes using UML class diagrams as personal note-taking while exploring unfamiliar code bases, particularly domain models. He observes that UML has fallen out of fashion but continues to find it valuable for his own use. He explicitly rejects reverse-engineering tools that auto-generate diagrams, arguing they produce useless output by including everything rather than highlighting what matters. The key distinction he draws is between diagrams as personal notes versus diagrams for communicating with others—different purposes requiring different approaches and tools.
key_points: UML remains useful as a personal note-taking tool even as it has fallen out of fashion in the industry
Reverse-engineering tools that auto-generate class diagrams are useless because they don't distinguish important from unimportant elements
Good diagrams highlight important stuff and ignore what's not—selective curation is the value
Speed matters for note-taking; Fowler prefers Umlet for its text-based wiki-ish markup
Diagrams for personal notes require different effort and tooling than diagrams meant to explain code to others
money_quotes: For me the value of a good diagram is that it highlights the important stuff, and ignores what's not.
I don't use reverse-engineering tools for this. Although this is quite straight-forward to do technically, the result is rather useless.
People who are less familiar with the syntax shouldn't worry about it in this mode of working.
With notes I'm creating reminders for myself, with explanatory diagrams I'm trying to communicate different things to another audience.
Although this isn't good for me financially, I can't say I'm displeased to see a lot of rather dodgy UMLisms going away.
key_insight: The value of a diagram lies not in completeness but in selective curation—highlighting what matters and ignoring what doesn't.
